claim: Between 86–97% of lost dogs in the US are eventually recovered, with a median recovery time of 2 days.
stance_target: most lost dogs are found — search efforts in the first 48–72 hours have the highest impact
scope_conditions: US dog-owning households, 2010 survey (Weiss et al. 2012), and Ohio county study (Lord et al. 2007b)
quoted_support: Research studies found that between 71-97% of lost dogs were recovered. Weiss et al. (2012) used statistics to estimate that between 86 to 97% of lost dogs in the US are found.

claim: 71% of recovered dogs are found less than 1 mile from the point of escape; 14% are found 1–5 miles away; only 7% are found more than 5 miles from home.
stance_target: initial search radius of 1 mile captures 71% of recoverable dogs; the active search perimeter should be extended to 5 miles if 1-mile searches fail
scope_conditions: 187 lost dogs in Montgomery County, Ohio, June–September 2005 (Lord et al. 2007b)
quoted_support: 8% returned home, 71% found less than one mile, 14% were found 1-5 miles, and 7% were found more than 5 miles from home.

claim: Neighborhood search is the dominant recovery method for lost dogs (49%), followed by dog returning on its own (20%), with shelter search accounting for only 2–12%.
stance_target: physical neighborhood canvassing is the highest-yield search intervention; shelter-only search is insufficient
scope_conditions: 1,015 US households surveyed 2010, Weiss et al. 2012
quoted_support: Most dogs were found by searching the neighborhood (49%, 38-59%). Followed by 20% (13-29%) returned home on their own. Only 6% (2-12%) were found by visiting or calling animal control.

claim: Dogs wearing identification at time of loss are recovered at 53% vs. 35% without ID — a significant difference (P<0.001).
stance_target: ID tagging/microchipping is an actionable intervention that increases recovery probability by ~50%
scope_conditions: 187 lost dogs in Montgomery County, Ohio, 2005
quoted_support: 53% of dogs with ID were found vs 35% of dogs without ID.

claim: 14% of dogs go missing in a 5-year period in the US; the commonly cited '1 in 3 pets will go missing in their lifetime' statistic has no published research support.
stance_target: the '33% lifetime missing' statistic used in fundraising/marketing is unsubstantiated
scope_conditions: US nationally representative telephone survey, 2010 (Weiss et al. 2012)
quoted_support: The only published research study on lost pet rates found that between 11-16% of dogs and 12 - 18% of cats are likely to go missing at least once in five years.

claim: Dogs are significantly more likely to be recovered when owners visit or call an animal agency (hazard ratio 1.8–2.1) and post neighborhood signs (effective after >7 days).
stance_target: parallel search actions — shelter notification AND neighborhood posting — maximise recovery odds
scope_conditions: 187 lost dogs in Ohio, 2005 (Lord et al. 2007b)
quoted_support: Owners were significantly more likely to recover their lost dogs when they called an animal agency (hazard ratio, 2.1), visited an animal agency (1.8), and posted neighborhood signs. However, posting signs did not become effective until the dog was lost for > 7 days.
